I found the requested items in your inbox. Summary:

1) Facebook security code
- Code: 463820
- Email sender: security@facebook.com
- Subject: "Your Facebook security code"
- Received: 2024-05-15 18:00:00
- Message (excerpt): "Your Facebook security code is: 463820 …"

2) TechServices password-reset link(s)
I found two password-reset emails received around the same time. Both contain reset links that point to techservices.com — one from the official-looking support address, the other from an alternate address that may be suspicious. Details:

- Email A (likely legitimate)
  - Link: https://techservices.com/auth/password-reset/token=?asfbuy3y2cdaqhvei
  - Sender: support@techservices.com
  - Subject: "TechServices Password Reset Request"
  - Received: 2024-05-19 23:50:00

- Email B (possible phishing / alternative source)
  - Link: https://techservices.com/auth/password-reset/token=?fjahwb24jab3
  - Sender: tech-services-password@aol.com
  - Subject: "Your TechServices password reset request"
  - Received: 2024-05-19 23:55:00

Security notes and recommendations
- Use the link from support@techservices.com (Email A) if you initiated the reset — it looks like the official sender. Treat the AOL-sender email (Email B) with caution; it could be a phishing attempt even though the URL text looks similar.
- Do not share the Facebook code publicly; only enter it on Facebook’s official site or app.
- If you did not request the password reset, ignore the reset links and consider changing your account passwords and enabling two-factor authentication.
- If you want, I can (a) search for additional related messages, (b) mark suspicious messages, or (c) move or delete those emails — tell me which action you’d like.
